description:
You are acting as an MLOps engineer who needs a tiny bit of automation to keep track of *.pkl* model artifacts produced by two experiments.  
    The workspace already contains the directory */home/user/experiments/* with two sub-folders:

      • /home/user/experiments/exp_alpha/  
      • /home/user/experiments/exp_beta/

    Inside each of those sub-folders there is exactly **one** Pickle file whose name ends with *.pkl*.  
    Your goal is to write a very small shell script (no more than 2-3 useful shell commands inside it, aside from the she-bang and possibly a comment) that, when executed, scans */home/user/experiments/*, extracts information about every *.pkl* file it finds, and writes a compact CSV summary to */home/user/artifacts_summary.log*.

    Implementation requirements
    1. Save the script as  /home/user/track_artifacts.sh  
       • It must start with the normal bash she-bang (#!/usr/bin/env bash).  
       • Keep the body to *approximately* two or three shell commands (pipes and redirections are fine).  
       • Make the script executable.

    2. When the user runs  
         /home/user/track_artifacts.sh  
       it must overwrite (not append to) */home/user/artifacts_summary.log* with exactly the following layout:

         Line 1 – header:      experiment,filename,filesize_bytes  
         Subsequent lines    one CSV line per *.pkl* file in lexicographical order of the experiment directory name.  
         
         • ‘experiment’   – just the name of the sub-directory (e.g. “exp_alpha”).  
         • ‘filename’     – the basename of the *.pkl* file (e.g. “model_alpha.pkl”).  
         • ‘filesize_bytes’ – the file’s size in bytes, as reported by stat -c%s.

    3. The output file must end with a newline.

    Example of the exact format that automated tests will verify (for the current sandbox contents):

        experiment,filename,filesize_bytes
        exp_alpha,model_alpha.pkl,19
        exp_beta,model_beta.pkl,34

    No extra spaces, no additional columns, and every line (including the last one) must end with a single newline (Unix line ending).

    Hints (do not reveal actual commands):
      • You’ll probably combine a single *find* (or *printf*) with a loop or *awk* to format the CSV.  
      • Remember to sort by the experiment directory names lexicographically before writing.

    Success criteria
      • /home/user/track_artifacts.sh exists, is executable, and contains no more than ~3 meaningful commands.  
      • Running the script generates /home/user/artifacts_summary.log with exactly the 3 lines shown above (header + two data lines) and nothing else.
truth:
Initial sandbox contents created by the test harness:

      Directory /home/user/experiments/exp_alpha/ contains
        – /home/user/experiments/exp_alpha/model_alpha.pkl  
          Content: "dummy model alpha\n"   (19 bytes total)

      Directory /home/user/experiments/exp_beta/ contains
        – /home/user/experiments/exp_beta/model_beta.pkl  
          Content: "dummy model beta with bigger size\n" (34 bytes total)

    Expected file after the agent has completed the task and executed the script:

      Path: /home/user/artifacts_summary.log  
      Exact byte-for-byte contents (including trailing newline):

        experiment,filename,filesize_bytes\n
        exp_alpha,model_alpha.pkl,19\n
        exp_beta,model_beta.pkl,34\n
name: task_000000_57427119
